MARLIE 1 — Lafayette, Louisiana
We invented the CHP Cascade Architecture — combined heat and power running a pure DC AI factory, no grid, no diesel, no inverters. This raise funds the first 5 MW at our owned Lafayette facility. First infrastructure. First revenue. First mover.

Louisiana’s first NVIDIA neocloud command center — built on Lafayette ground, powered by Lafayette gas, connected by LUS Fiber, and operated by a Lafayette-licensed general contractor. DSX-compliant, NVIDIA liquid-cooled, running Dynamo inference OS. This is Lafayette building its own AI infrastructure — and inviting its utility partners to be part of it from day one. Shovel-ready. Federally eligible. Act 730 qualified. H2 2026.

Why Now

The technologies existed separately. We connected them.

Combined Heat and Power has run offshore platforms for decades. Chip-to-chip liquid cooling just matured with NVIDIA's NVL72. The Munters desiccant system has controlled humidity in industrial environments for years. 800V DC bus architecture just reached data center grade. None of these are new. What's new is connecting all of them — in a standard 8×40 ISO container — into a self-contained AI compute node that needs no grid, no air conditioning, and no external cooling plant. We saw it because we ran these systems offshore. We're deploying it because the timing is finally right.

No Air Conditioning
Munters desiccant system controls atmosphere inside each cassette. Zero HVAC overhead. Traditional data centers burn 40 cents of every dollar fighting heat.
CHP Loop Closed
Chip-level liquid cooling pulls heat off the GPU as hot liquid. That thermal output feeds back into the CHP cycle. Waste heat becomes a resource, not a problem.
Off Grid by Design
Natural gas is the fuel. Louisiana has more of it than any state in the country. No grid dependency. No utility negotiations. No transmission delays. Deploy and run.
Proven In The Field
Gensets, heat recovery, modular skid architecture — all of it has run on offshore platforms in Cat 4 conditions. We're not testing anything. We're deploying what works.
Autonomous AI DC Cassette — Front Elevation
The Moat

Lafayette Neural Network — 100 MW Across 10 Nodes. 5-Mile Radius.

MARLIE 1 is not a single data center. It is the first node of a distributed AI infrastructure network spanning Lafayette's critical institutions. 10 sites. 10 MW each. Each site independent, each site connected. If one goes down, the others carry the load. No single point of failure. No grid stress. Small footprint deployments, each on natural gas, each running the same proven cassette architecture.

MARLIE 1 — Hub
1201 SE Evangeline Thruway
Owned property. Anchor node. First 5 MW funded by this raise. Scales to 20 MW. Command and control for the full network.
Trappeys — 200 MW
112,500 sq ft · 4 Buildings
Historic cannery. $151M Riverfront District anchor. Largest node in the network. 500 racks. 2.05 MW solar offset. I-10/I-49 logistics access.
Lafayette Regional Airport
KLFT — < 1 Mile from MARLIE 1
Autonomous drone hub. FAA Part 107 active. Emergency response AI. Airport AI ground traffic. NVIDIA Metropolis integration target.
UL Lafayette
University AI compute. Research partnership. Dr. Ramesh Kolluru — new president, CS PhD, R1 builder. GPU infrastructure gap identified.
Lafayette General Hospital
Medical AI compute. HIPAA-grade isolation. On-site processing — patient data never leaves the building. Priority customer tier.
7 Additional Sites
10 sites identified within the network radius. Schools, first responders, SMBs, government. Each takes 10 MW. Each independent. All interconnected.
The distributed model is the grid strategy. We are not putting 100 MW in one location and stressing the utility. We are placing 10 MW nodes at 10 sites across a 5-mile radius — each behind its own natural gas supply, each off-grid, each connected by fiber. The network is inherently resilient. The footprint is inherently acceptable. The infrastructure is inherently local. This is how Lafayette becomes the first city in Louisiana with a distributed AI utility.
The Stack

Every component proven. First time assembled this way.

01
8×40 ISO Container Cassette
Standard shipping container. 10 NVIDIA racks. Stackable. Deployable anywhere with a gas line. The most proven enclosure format on earth.
02
Chip-to-Chip Liquid Cooling
NVIDIA NVL72 direct liquid cooling. Heat leaves as hot liquid, not hot air. The breakthrough that makes CHP viable in this context.
03
CHP — Natural Gas Power
Combined heat and power. Gas in, electricity and recoverable heat out. Bloom SOFC primary. Cat G3520H bridge. Louisiana gas lines survived every hurricane on record.
04
Munters Atmosphere Control
Desiccant dehumidification. No air conditioning. No chiller plant. No cooling tower. Autonomously controlled. The HVAC budget goes to zero.
05
800V DC Bus — OCP Stage 1d
Pure DC microgrid. No AC conversion losses. OCP-compliant architecture. 97% efficiency from source to rack. The power standard the industry is moving to.
06
NVIDIA DSX — Full Stack
DSX-compliant. Dynamo inference OS. NVIDIA NVL72 Vera Rubin racks. Omniverse digital twin. The full software stack running on hardware we own.
The Founder

Scott Tomsu speaks every language this project requires.

25 years as an ROV Superintendent running critical systems in conditions that shut down most data centers permanently. FAA Private Pilot. Louisiana GC with three state contractor licenses. Drag racing champion. Commercial diver. He's run these generators offshore. He's managed this equipment in Cat 4 conditions. He designed the electrical. He wrote the software stack. He's having active conversations with NVIDIA.

This is not a team being assembled. This is one operator who has already crossed every domain this project touches — from the contractor pulling permits to the GPU architect specifying racks. He decides what layer he's talking to and meets them there.

The Mission
This is a Louisiana startup building AI infrastructure for Louisiana. Not a hedge fund. Not a hyperscaler. A family going all in on what's coming — before anyone else here understands what's on the horizon. Scott's daughter is coming in. His brother is coming in. The network serves the school down the street, the hospital up the road, the airport next door. This is not an exit strategy. This is a community being built.

Power Strategy
BLOOM SOFC
PRIMARY
Pure DC microgrid — no grid, no AC anywhere
Marlie I runs on 40 Bloom Energy SOFC units — 10 MW, 800V DC direct. Natural gas in, 800V DC out — no inverter, no rectifier, no conversion loss. 54% electrical efficiency, best-in-class for on-site generation. Henry Hub — the world’s most liquid natural gas benchmark — sits 40 miles away in Erath, LA, locking fuel costs to the most competitive gas price on earth. First Solar TR1 panels offset daytime load. Eaton xStorage battery provides instant ATS bridge. The grid is never used for consumption at Marlie I. Five layers. Bloom first. Hurricane-rated. Pure DC.

Path A — This Page
Bloom SOFC + Solar

The premium architecture. Pure DC from source to rack — no inverter, no rectifier, no AC ever. Highest upfront capital, best electrical efficiency at 54%, cleanest operating profile. Designed for permanent, owned infrastructure.

Path B — CHP Genset
Natural Gas Generator + CHP Cascade

Lower capital entry point. Combined heat and power recovers waste heat from the genset exhaust — that recovered heat drives the liquid cooling loop directly, which changes the efficiency math significantly. Competitive with Bloom on a total-energy basis at scale. Best fit for rapid deployment and edge sites.

POWER — 10 MW — Pure DC Microgrid — No Grid. No AC. No Diesel.

Marlie I runs on a pure 800V DC microgrid. Natural gas in, 800V DC out — direct to the NVIDIA DSX rack bus. No inverter. No rectifier. No conversion loss. Bloom SOFC is primary. First Solar TR1 offsets daytime load. Eaton xStorage LFP bridges transitions. The grid is never used for compute power at Marlie I. Louisiana gas infrastructure has survived every Cat 4 storm on record without interruption. This power stack does not depend on transmission lines.

NVIDIA DSX COMPLIANT
OCP STAGE 1d
800V DC NATIVE
NO GRID
NO AC
NO DIESEL

COOLING — Full Liquid Stack — GPU Die to Heat Rejection — Gulf Coast Hardened

Every NVL72 rack at Marlie I runs 100% liquid cooled end-to-end — from the Boyd cold plate at the GPU die through the CoolIT row CDU to facility heat rejection. No air cooling anywhere in the compute loop. Louisiana's Gulf Coast climate runs 75–80% relative humidity 8 months a year. The Munters HCD desiccant system is integrated from day one to manage moisture at the cassette and building level, preventing condensation on cold cooling lines and protecting electronics from the environment.

100% LIQUID COOLED
ZERO AIR COOLING
GULF COAST HARDENED
NVIDIA RVL / AVL
LIQUID COOLING CHAIN — GPU DIE TO FACILITY LOOP
GPU DIE
Boyd Cold Plate
Copper · NVIDIA RVL certified · ~700W per GPU captured at die
RACK
Staubli UQD Quick-Disconnects
450 connections per NVL72 rack · dry-break, no spillage · hot-swap capable
ROW CDU
CoolIT CHx2000
2 MW per unit · 12 NVL72 racks · hot-swap pumps · NVIDIA RVL/AVL · 6-nines availability
PIPING
Danfoss CoolTrain TCS
Pre-fabricated valve train · factory pre-tested · bolt-together on site · eliminates field pipe fab
REJECT
Adiabatic Dry Cooler
Exterior heat rejection · adiabatic assist for Louisiana summer peaks >95°F
HUMIDITY CONTROL — MUNTERS HCD DESICCANT — PHASED IN

Lafayette averages 75–80% relative humidity eight months a year. Cold liquid cooling lines in a high-humidity environment create condensation risk that destroys electronics. The Munters HCD desiccant dehumidifier controls interior humidity with no compressor — approximately 500W draw — maintaining positive pressure to keep moist outside air from infiltrating the compute environment. Precision control to ±1°F dew point. Phased into each cassette as deployed. Waste heat from Bloom SOFC reactivates the desiccant wheel at no additional energy cost.

MUNTERS HCD
NO COMPRESSOR

WASTE HEAT REACTIVATION

Dew point precision control
~500W
System draw — no compressor
0
Reactivation energy cost — uses Bloom waste heat
130 kW

Per-rack TDP supported by the Delta 140 kW CDU (NVIDIA NVL72 certified, RVL/AVL listed). 45°C supply / 55–60°C return. Hot water loop — usable for waste heat recovery downstream. Delta Electronics manufactured in Plano, Texas.

GROQ LPU — INFERENCE ACCELERATION

Groq Language Processing Units (LPUs) are purpose-built inference engines — not trained on Groq hardware, but run on Groq hardware at speeds no GPU can match for deterministic single-stream inference workloads. Marlie I integrates Groq LPUs alongside NVIDIA NVL72 racks to give customers a choice of inference platform. GPU clusters handle training, fine-tuning, and high-throughput batch inference. Groq handles latency-sensitive, real-time, and edge-routed workloads. Same DC power bus. Same management plane. Complementary — not competing.

GROQ LPU
DETERMINISTIC INFERENCE
LOW LATENCY
800V DC COMPATIBLE
GPU
Training + batch inference + fine-tuning — NVIDIA NVL72
LPU
Real-time inference — deterministic latency — Groq
One DC Power Bus. Two Inference Engines. Full Workload Coverage.

INVESTMENT THESIS
01
Skip Blackwell. Deploy Rubin.

The market is building AI facilities around Blackwell — last generation's answer. Marlie I is designed from the ground up for NVIDIA Vera Rubin, shipping H2 2026. Rubin delivers 2.5x the FP4 compute density of GB200 NVL72 (3.6 vs 1.44 ExaFLOPS per rack), 22 TB/s HBM4 memory bandwidth per GPU, and 24x faster rack deployment. Investors who move now own the next generation of inference infrastructure — not a commodity rack of yesterday's hardware.

02
Infrastructure Already Exists

1201 SE Evangeline Thruway is not a greenfield project. startup already owns the building. LUS Fiber is on site — live. The LUS power substation is 1 mile out. Natural gas access via Atmos Energy — on site. The property carries only $15K in total debt. Site prep, permits, and GC licensing are in hand. We are not buying land and hoping. We are converting an existing, debt-free asset into a revenue-generating AI factory.

03
Money Is Already Flowing

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act allocates $500B toward domestic AI infrastructure. Louisiana's Act 730 provides a 20-year state and local tax exemption on qualifying manufacturing equipment. The BEAD Broadband Program and Gulf OCS Revenue sharing add further federal pathways. Marlie I is positioned at the intersection of every major capital flow into domestic AI — 100% US ownership, Texas-manufactured NVIDIA hardware, Louisiana-licensed GC. The funding is not hypothetical. We qualify.

Government Funding Stack — Federal + State + Local
Federal
One Big Beautiful Bill Act

Signed July 4, 2025, the OBBBA creates the most favorable federal policy environment for domestic AI infrastructure in US history. It codifies FEOC (Foreign Entity of Concern) restrictions — any project with Chinese, Russian, Iranian, or North Korean supply chain involvement is disqualified from federal tax credits, grants, and incentives. Marlie I is purpose-built for full compliance: NVIDIA hardware manufactured in Texas (Foxconn Houston, Wistron Fort Worth), 100% US ownership, Louisiana-licensed GC. The OBBBA also added $500M to the BEAD broadband program and raised Gulf OCS revenue sharing to $650M/year — both of which directly benefit Louisiana AI infrastructure. The $500B Stargate public-private AI program runs parallel to the OBBBA; Marlie I's FEOC-clean structure qualifies it for Stargate-aligned federal procurement and financing channels.

Federal
BEAD Broadband Program

The Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment (BEAD) Program distributes federal funding to expand broadband infrastructure in underserved areas. Lafayette sits within a region with significant BEAD allocation. AI factories require massive bandwidth — and the presence of LUS Fiber, a city-owned gigabit network, creates a unique opportunity to layer BEAD-funded backhaul expansion directly into the Marlie I connectivity plan. LUS Fiber is on site at Marlie I — live and connected. The AI factory accelerates BEAD project eligibility for the City of Lafayette while simultaneously solving Marlie I's connectivity requirements.

Federal
Gulf OCS Revenue

The OBBBA raised Louisiana's annual Gulf of America OCS revenue-sharing cap from $500M to $650M/year through 2034 — a 30% increase in the state's offshore energy dividend. Louisiana's share (~$200M/year at cap) funds the Coastal Master Plan, the infrastructure backbone that protects sites like Marlie I from long-term flood risk. Simultaneously, Louisiana's offshore energy production — the same natural gas supply chain feeding Marlie I's generators — is the economic engine behind this revenue. Henry Hub, the world's most liquid natural gas trading point, is 40 miles from Marlie I in Erath, LA. Marlie I's fuel costs are benchmarked to the most competitive gas price on earth.

State
Louisiana Act 730

Louisiana Act 730 (effective July 1, 2024) provides a 20-year state and local sales and use tax rebate on qualifying AI factory equipment, software, construction materials, and installation — with a 10-year renewal option (30 years total). Qualification requires $200M+ in capital investment and 50+ new permanent jobs certified by Louisiana Economic Development. Marlie I's capital plan is structured to meet both thresholds. Once certified, a Direct Payment Number eliminates tax liability on the single largest cost in the project: GPU hardware. Stackable with the Quality Jobs Program (6% payroll rebate, 10 years) and LED FastStart (free customized workforce training). Combined with Louisiana's #1-ranked industrial electricity rates, Act 730 makes Marlie I's per-token economics unbeatable.

startup Compliance Note

Federal funding under the OBBBA requires strict domestic content and zero involvement by prohibited foreign entities. Marlie I is purpose-built for compliance — NVIDIA hardware manufactured in Texas (Foxconn Houston, Wistron Fort Worth), 100% US ownership, US-licensed general contractor. We are not just eligible — we are the ideal candidate.

The startup Ecosystem — Beyond the AI Factory
⚡
Self-Powered + Grid Feed

Marlie I runs on Bloom SOFC fuel cells as primary power. Solar and LFP battery bridge are integrated into the 800V DC microgrid. No grid connection. No diesel. AI factories live or die on power availability — Marlie I owns its power stack.

ENERGY INDEPENDENT
🚗
EV Charging Substation

Expansion plans include a commercial EV charging substation on-site. Lafayette's proximity to I-10 and US-90 makes the location ideal for fleet charging infrastructure. The substation leverages existing power capacity from the natural gas generation system, adding a high-margin ancillary revenue stream without additional land acquisition.

SITE TWO EXPANSION
✈
Airport Adjacent — Part 107 UAS

Marlie I sits adjacent to Lafayette Regional Airport (LFT). startup holds a FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate, enabling commercial UAS operations from the site. Drone-based infrastructure inspection, aerial survey, and last-mile logistics represent near-term revenue opportunities that leverage the site's location and existing certifications — no additional real estate required.

UAS / DRONE READY
🔒
Physical Security — Built In

AI factory physical security is not an afterthought at Marlie I — it is part of the original buildout design. Perimeter hardening, access control, and 24/7 monitoring infrastructure are included in the initial buildout scope. Colocation and managed inference customers require SOC 2-equivalent physical controls. Marlie I meets that bar from day one, enabling premium-tier customer contracts without post-hoc security retrofits.

SECURED FACILITY
🌐
Hub & Spoke — Louisiana Network

Marlie I is designed as the hub of a Louisiana AI network. Spoke facilities in Baton Rouge, New Orleans, and Lake Charles extend inference capacity to enterprise customers across the state. LUS Fiber and existing Louisiana broadband infrastructure provide the backbone. This hub-and-spoke model scales revenue without proportional capital expenditure — each spoke builds on Marlie I's existing operations team, software stack, and customer relationships.

SCALABLE NETWORK
📡
LUS Fiber — City-Owned Network

LUS Fiber is one of the few municipal gigabit networks in the United States — owned and operated by the City of Lafayette. On site at Marlie I, LUS Fiber provides direct, low-latency connection to city infrastructure, state government networks, and regional enterprise customers. City ownership means no incumbent telco gatekeeping, competitive pricing, and a natural public-private partnership pathway for Marlie I's connectivity buildout.

DIRECT FIBER CONNECTION
Site & Infrastructure — 1201 SE Evangeline Thruway
The Building Today 1201 SE Evangeline Thruway — Lafayette, LA

Future home of MARLIE 1. Metal-clad industrial structure on owned land, built by Scott Tomsu 20 years ago. High ceilings, reinforced slab foundation, roll-up bay access for equipment delivery, frontage on SE Evangeline Thruway. Zero structural damage through multiple Gulf hurricanes. The bones of an AI factory — already standing.

Owner-Built Infrastructure

Scott Tomsu designed and built this building himself, 20 years ago. Exterior footprint: 24 ft × 40 ft. Interior working floor: 22 ft × 35 ft — 770 sq ft of compute space (downstairs), plus a full second floor and the adjoining property. The ceiling is 2×12 framing with two layers of 5/8″ Type X sheetrock — a UL-rated fire assembly, built that way from day one because the shop was originally a motorcycle garage. Heavy insulation throughout creates thermal stability ideal for optimizing liquid cooling airflow between rack cabinets and rails. It has survived multiple direct Gulf hurricane impacts with zero structural damage. Reinforced slab foundation. Built on high ground in the Lafayette city core — above regional base flood elevation, in FEMA Zone X. This is not a leased warehouse or a speculative build. It is a proven, battle-tested structure on owned land, built by the same hands that will deploy the AI factory inside it. The GC license, the building, and the certifications are all one team.
